perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees (i) that it will not disclose to any third party or use any Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other except as expressly permitted in this Agreement and (ii) that it will take all reasonable measures to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information of the other party in its possession or control, which will in no event be less than the measures it uses to maintain the confidentiality of its own information of similar importance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees (i) that it will not disclose to any third party or use any Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other except as permitted in this Agreement and (ii) that it will make efforts to maintain the confidentiality of all Confidential Information of the other party in its possession or control.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. Replacing 'expressly permitted' with 'permitted' broadens the scope of allowable disclosures. Changing 'take all reasonable measures' to 'make efforts' introduces ambiguity, making it less enforceable. The original clause also included the specification that the measures would be no less than those used to protect their own information. This has also been removed.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified language, using 'make efforts,' is vague and does not ensure that reasonable measures are taken, creating ambiguity around the level of protection required and potentially falling short of the legal standard. Deleting the clause that states the measures will be no less than those used to protect their own information weakens the clause further.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially falling short of legal standards. The original text required 'all reasonable measures' to maintain confidentiality, but the changed text only requires 'make efforts'. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation', where the ambiguous obligation makes it difficult to enforce under state or national law. According to the law explanation, CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, and the modified language does not ensure that reasonable measures are taken.

perturbation.original_text: In no event will either party be liable to the other for any special, incidental or consequential damages, whether based on breach of contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise, whether or not that party has been advised of the possibility of such damage.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party will be fully liable to the other for any special, incidental or consequential damages, whether based on breach of contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise, whether or not that party has been advised of the possibility of such damage.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'fully', the contract now implies that parties can be partially liable for special, incidental, or consequential damages. This ambiguity leaves room for interpretation on the degree of liability, making it difficult to enforce the clause effectively and contradicting the intent of a clear liability limitation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2719 allows for the limitation or exclusion of consequential damages unless the limitation is unconscionable. The word 'fully' creates uncertainty that challenges the enforecability of completely limiting or excluding consequential damages, as it is open to interpretation of what would be defined as being 'fully' liable. The code aims for predictability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2719 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 7. Remedies [2701 - 2725] ( Chapter 7 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2719. (1) Subject to the provisions of subdivisions (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) The agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this division and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this division, as by limiting the buyer’s remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of nonconforming goods or parts; and (b) Resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this code. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is invalid unless it is proved that the limitation is not unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages where the loss is commercial is va
perturbation.justification: The modification, by inserting "fully," introduces ambiguity regarding the extent of liability for special, incidental, or consequential damages. This directly undermines the principle outlined in California Commercial Code § 2719, which, as interpreted by the LLM and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows for the limitation or exclusion of consequential damages. The addition of 'fully' opens the door for partial liability, conflicting with the intent of clearly limiting or excluding such damages as contemplated by the code. The ambiguity makes the contract difficult to enforce effectively and contradicts the aim for predictability within commercial agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Application Provider will not serve advertising on the Co-Branded Application that (i) violates any law, rule, or regulation, (ii) relates to pornography, gaming, tobacco, or alcohol, (iii) is the subject of a claim to trademark, trade name, service mark, or other proprietary rights, or Excite@Home may request removal of any such advertising from the Co-Branded Application, and Application Provider shall remove any such advertising within twenty-four (24) hours of receipt of any such written request.
perturbation.changed_text: Application Provider will endeavor not to serve advertising on the Co-Branded Application that (i) violates any law, rule, or regulation, (ii) relates to pornography, gaming, tobacco, or alcohol, (iii) is the subject of a claim to trademark, trade name, service mark, or other proprietary rights, or Excite@Home may request removal of any such advertising from the Co-Branded Application, and Application Provider will attempt to remove any such advertising within a reasonable time of receipt of any such written request.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will not serve' with 'will endeavor not to serve' makes the obligation less strict. Similarly, replacing 'shall remove' with 'will attempt to remove within a reasonable time' introduces ambiguity about the timeliness and certainty of the removal. This creates a weaker, less enforceable obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act prohibits the use of trademarks in a way that is likely to cause confusion, or to deceive. The original clause strictly prohibited advertisements that could cause trademark infringement claims. By softening the language, the new clause is ambigious on whether the Application provider should endeavor not to host ads that could potentially infringe on trademarks and intellectual property.

Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will not serve' to 'will endeavor not to serve' and 'shall remove' to 'will attempt to remove within a reasonable time' significantly weakens the obligation on the Application Provider regarding advertising that infringes on trademarks, creating an Ambiguous Legal Obligation. The Lanham Act, as suggested by the Law Explanation, aims to prevent trademark infringement. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 15 USC 1114, discusses remedies for trademark infringement, indicating the importance of preventing such infringement. The original text provided a clear obligation to avoid and remove infringing ads, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity, potentially leading to continued infringement and thus contradicting the intent to protect trademarks as suggested by the LLM generated law explanation.
